questions.title: Two Sum
questions.question: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of two different numbers such that they add up to target. You may assume exactly one valid answer exists, and the same element cannot be used twice.
questions.expected_approach: Use a hash map from value to index. For each number, check whether target - number already exists in the map.

questions.title: Valid Parentheses
questions.question: Given a string containing only the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine whether the brackets are valid. A valid string closes every opening bracket using the same type and in the correct order.

examples.explanation: Every opening bracket is closed by the same type in the correct order.
The closing brackets do not match the most recent unmatched opening brackets.

questions.expected_approach: Push opening brackets onto a stack. For each closing bracket, pop and verify that it matches. The string is valid only if the stack is empty at the end.

questions.title: Maximum Subarray Sum
questions.question: Given an integer array nums, find the maximum possible sum of a non-empty contiguous subarray.

examples.explanation: The subarray [4, -1, 2, 1] has the largest sum: 6.
When all values are negative, choose the largest single element.

questions.expected_approach: Use Kadane's algorithm. Track the best subarray ending at the current index and the global best sum.

questions.title: Search In Rotated Sorted Array
questions.question: Given a sorted array that has been rotated at an unknown pivot and an integer target, return the index of target if it exists, otherwise return -1. All values in the array are unique.
constraints: nums is sorted in ascending order before rotation

examples.explanation: The target 0 is present at index 4.
The target 3 is not present in the array.

questions.expected_approach: Use modified binary search. At every step, identify which half is sorted and decide whether the target can lie in that half.

questions.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
questions.question: Given a string s, return the length of the longest substring that contains no repeated characters.
constraints: s may contain letters, digits, symbols, and spaces

examples.explanation: The answer is "abc", with length 3.
The longest substring without repeating characters is "b".

questions.expected_approach: Use a sliding window with a map from character to its latest index. Move the left boundary whenever a repeated character appears inside the current window.

questions.title: Number Of Islands
questions.question: Given an m x n grid of characters where '1' represents land and '0' represents water, count the number of islands. An island is formed by horizontally or vertically connected land cells.
constraints: Only horizontal and vertical connections count

examples.explanation: There is one island on the upper-left and one island on the right/bottom side.
Diagonal land cells are not connected, so each land cell is its own island.

questions.expected_approach: Scan every cell. When unvisited land is found, run DFS or BFS to mark the whole island, then increment the island count.
questions.space_complexity: O(m * n) in the worst case for recursion or queue storage

questions.title: Merge Intervals
questions.question: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [start, end], merge all overlapping intervals and return a list of non-overlapping intervals that cover all input intervals.

examples.explanation: [1,3] and [2,6] overlap, so they merge into [1,6].
Touching intervals are treated as overlapping.

questions.expected_approach: Sort intervals by start time, then iterate once while maintaining the last merged interval.

questions.title: Kth Largest Element In An Array
questions.question: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the kth largest element in the array. The kth largest element is based on sorted order, not distinct values.

examples.explanation: The sorted order is [6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1], so the 2nd largest is 5.
Duplicates count separately, so the 4th largest value is 4.

questions.expected_approach: Use a min-heap of size k, or use quickselect for expected linear time.
questions.time_complexity: O(n log k) with heap, O(n) expected with quickselect
questions.space_complexity: O(k) with heap, O(1) extra with in-place quickselect

questions.title: LRU Cache
questions.question: Design a data structure that supports get(key) and put(key, value) in O(1) average time. When the cache exceeds its capacity, evict the least recently used key.
constraints: At most 200000 get and put operations will be called

examples.input: capacity = 2; put(1,1); put(2,2); get(1); put(3,3); get(2); put(4,4); get(1); get(3); get(4)
examples.explanation: Keys 2 and 1 are evicted as they become least recently used.
With capacity 1, inserting key 20 evicts key 10.

questions.expected_approach: Combine a hash map for key lookup with a doubly linked list for recency order. Move accessed or updated nodes to the front.

questions.title: Word Ladder
questions.question: Given beginWord, endWord, and a wordList, return the length of the shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord. Each step can change exactly one character, and every intermediate word must exist in wordList.
constraints: All words contain lowercase English letters
All words have the same length

examples.explanation: One shortest path is hit -> hot -> dot -> dog -> cog.
The end word is not in the word list, so no valid transformation exists.

questions.expected_approach: Use BFS because each transformation has equal cost. Generate neighboring words by changing one character at a time, and use a set for unvisited words.
questions.time_complexity: O(n * m * 26), where n is wordList size and m is word length
